data.title: Fair Isaac: Score Prices Shall Keep Rising
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO) article "FICO: A Buy On Pullbacks" published in November 2023 with a buy rating, the stock has more than doubled its share price due to the outstanding performance of the business and a multiple expansion.

As stated in the comment section of the previous article, I sold half of my position at $2,350, given the share price was too far away from my estimate of the fair price on a base case scenario, which I am updating in this article.

Although I consider the company to be slightly overvalued, I don't believe in selling the highest-quality companies (and FICO ranks #1 in my watchlist quality wise). That is why I keep half of my position (around 12% of my portfolio) and will increase again if the share price falls below its fair value.

Company Overview

FICO operates in two business segments, scores and software. While both segments have outstanding margins and low need to reinvest due to their asset light nature (D&A was 0.75% of FY2024's revenues), the worst of its segments is the software one with operating margins above 30%. The score segment operating margins have ranged between 86% and 90% during the last four quarters, and it enjoys significant operating leverage.

Scores Segment

FICO owns the proprietary algorithm applied to consumers' data that generates the FICO Score (a three-digit number ranging from 300 to 850 that determines the customer's credit quality).

Since the data is collected and managed by the consumer reporting agencies (Experian, TransUnion, and Equifax), the only costs for FICO are the ones related to improving the predictability of its scores (the model has been updated several times since its first version was released in 1989).

The cost of issuing an extra score is zero, and FICO doesn't need to deal with the final customer, since banks, credit card issuers, mortgage lenders and auto loan originators deal with the credit bureaus, paying a percentage of the fee to FICO in exchange of applying its algorithm.

Most of the revenues on the score segment are generated B2B (∼77%), and FICO's fee represents a minimal cost compared to the overall cost of a lending process.

The main strengths of the scores segment are the network effects, and the fact it has become an industry standard on loan originations. On top of that, when a mortgage lender wants to sell the loan to the GSE (Government Sponsored Agencies like Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac), it is mandatory to provide the FICO score.

Mortgage originations represent 34% of the scores' revenue (Q1 2025), and FICO has recently announced a price increase from $3.5 in 2024 to $4.95 in 2025. That is a 41% price increase on top of many previous price increases. From the $0.5-$0.6 per pull charged in 2018, I've approximated the mortgage score price per year, obtaining a CAGR of over 38%.

The reasoning behind this price increases is the high value provided by the FICO score and the fact the company has held it at abnormal low prices for decades. Given the strong competitive position, and the low price compared to the overall mortgage origination fees, the company has been able to push those price increases.

In the expected growth section, I will discuss how much I believe the company will be able to increase its prices, which, in my opinion, is the most important factor in determining its fair price and the future growth.

Scores Segment Recent Performance

During the last year (Q2 2024 to Q1 2025), FICO has generated $963.3M in revenues in the scores segment, with a YoY increase of 23% in its last quarter.

The most notable developments by sub-segments are:

  • Mortgage origination revenues were up 110% YoY.
  • Auto origination revenues were up 5% YoY.
  • Credit card and personal loan revenues were down 3% YoY.
  • B2C revenues were up 3% YoY.

Note that even the mortgage originations have doubled YoY, this series of data presents significant volatility, and it needs to be taken with a grain of salt. Despite this, mortgage originations have increased after a softer market due to the initial rate increases in 2022, and it seems to be normalizing.

If the trend continues its current course, and given the recent price increase, I expect significant growth in the B2B side of the business.

On the B2C side of the business, even the company doesn't disclose the exact revenues generated, by reviewing past earnings calls one can see that the variance is much lower, mainly driven by the number of pulls from individual customers through the myFICO.com website.

Another relevant development during the last months has been the increasing adoption of FICO 10T (which is expected to be required for government backed mortgages sold to the GSEs, but there is no specific timeline). Recently, non-government backed mortgages utilizing FICO 10T scoring model began trading on MCT Marketplace, the largest U.S. mortgage secondary market. I believe this is a highly relevant development that keeps the FICO models as the industry standard.

Software Segment

While the scores part of FICO's business has been relatively linear over the decades product-wise, the other segments have been changing significantly with some divestitures and new business lines.

The software segment provides analytics, decision-making, and fraud detection. The company has transitioned from having a long list of applications to an integrated cloud-based platform where customers can bring data together from multiple sources and tailor its configuration to a high degree based on their needs. Currently, the FICO Platform generates 31% of its annual recurring revenues (ARR).

The main drivers for growth in this segment are the increase in the number of users (mainly banks, insurers, lenders, large retailers, government and public agencies, telecommunications providers, and automotive companies) and the increase in usage, since FICO subscribes multi-year contracts with partial payments based on the number of accounts, transactions or decisions use cases deployed.

Software Segment Recent Performance

During the last quarter, the growth in the software segment has reduced its pace, as shown in the image below:

The ARR on the platform segment is currently growing at 20%, which is significantly lower compared to the previous quarters, bringing the overall software growth rates to 6% YoY (8% if eliminating the currency impact), generating annual revenues of $729M.

The 6% growth has been mainly driven by the increase in usage for current customers (∼83% of the increase), and the rest has been driven by an increase in customer base.

In my personal view, this is the most negative development during the last months, since the increase in usage is growing slower along with the customer base growth.

Regarding this part of the business, during the last earnings call the management stated the following:

I wouldn't read too much into any particular quarter. This quarter, we got dinged a little bit with foreign exchange. But really, the biggest driver is the fact that it flows through from bookings and we had some weaker bookings quarters in the past. We haven't revised our view about the kind of long-term growth trajectory for the platform business, which is in the 30s, 30% or so. And so I wouldn't read too much into this quarter's number.

(William Lansing, CEO)

While the management expects ARR to accelerate in the coming quarters, and I have FICO's management in high regard due to its conservative guidance and proven track record of delivering results, I will keep track of the growth rates in the software segment during the next quarters but will adjust my model's assumptions.

Transition from tri-merge to bi-merge

The transition from the tri-merge reporting when applying for a mortgage to the bi-merge report has been discussed for a long time. In 2022, the Federal Housing Finance Authority (FHFA) announced they are planning to shift from requiring three credit reports (one for every agency) to requiring only two but providing on top of the FICO 10T Score, also the Vantage 4.0 (a model developed by the three major credit bureaus).

While it was initially planned for implementation during the first quarter of 2024, it has been constantly delayed, and the FHFA has recently announced there is no planned timeline.

The intention for this change was to increase competition between the credit bureaus, but the industry fears that providing only two reports would reduce the visibility of the customer's credit risk, and its implementation would have a transition that could increase costs for borrowers.

One of the key competitive advantages of FICO and the credit agencies is the reduced cost compared to the mortgage closing costs.

According to a report released by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau in September 2023, the average closing cost for a mortgage was around $6,000.

A tri-merger report costs around $90 ($30 per report), of which $14.85 will go to FICO in 2025. This is a 1.5% total cost for the borrower, and FICO is charging 0.25% of the mortgage closing costs.

Given the relatively low percentage charged by FICO and the three bureaus, I believe the savings provided by this transition doesn't compensate for the costs.

If the bi-merge report was implemented, after the transition period the number of FICO scores would temporarily decrease by about 33%, which is the price increase for one year. In that scenario, it'd be important to follow the magnitude of the stock price drop to see if the market is exaggerating the consequences.

Expected Growth and Valuation

As stated before, I consider the current price to be overvalued, and in this section, I will present my assumptions in a base case scenario, along with an optimistic and a pessimistic scenario along with its chances of occurring.

Base Case Scenario

In my base case scenario, I make the following assumptions:

  • B2C Scores growing at 3.5%: in line with inflation with modest volume growth.
  • B2B Score price growing at 14.5% CAGR (reaching 0.5% of mortgage closing costs in 2032, assuming a 3.5% increase in mortgage closing costs per year).
  • B2B volumes continuing its accelerated growth given the low comparables and normalizing in 3 years.
  • The software segment growing at 5% per year.
  • Margin increase in scores and software due to operating leverage.
  • A tax rate decrease to 15% between 2026 and 2029, and increase again from 2030.
  • An 8.5% discount rate.
  • A 4% growth in perpetuity and 25x FCF exit multiple.
  • A 14% decrease in shares outstanding during the next 8 years, given buybacks are the main use for FICO's excess free cash flow.

In my base case scenario, I assume no further regulations or limits in price increases, and growth rates that, I believe, are neutral in B2B volume growth and software.

The long-term growth rate is consistent with inflation, and the FCF exit multiple is reasonable for a company with FICO's characteristics but assuming a much slower growth.

The current average fair price between the exit multiple and growth in perpetuity approaches is $1,510, implying the stock is currently overvalued by 20%.

Under these assumptions, the stock would have a share price of $3,071 in 2032, providing an 8% yearly return from current prices, which is significantly below my hurdle rate.

Optimistic and Pessimistic Scenarios

On an optimistic scenario, I expect the score segment to grow at 4% per year and the B2B segment to increase its prices to 0.75% of the total mortgage closing price (reaching $19 per score in 2023 from the current $4.95). The volume growth I used in this scenario is also higher, with 25%, 15%, and 10% during the next three years.

Software operating margins would reach 35.5% (compared to the 34.5% in the base case) given a faster revenue growth at 7% per year.

Applying an 8.5% discount rate, a 30x FCF exit multiple, and a 4% long-term growth rate, the average fair price per share would be at $3,150, showing a significant discount to the current price of $1,800.

Management has been asked many times about what price mortgage scores should reach compared to closing costs, and they haven't provided any specific figure so far. What they have commented is that they don't see any elasticity at this point, and I see a real possibility of bringing the prices between 0.5% and 1% of mortgage origination fees.

On the other hand, in a pessimistic scenario, the company would become regulated with a limit on its yearly price increases, something similar to the VeriSign (VRSN) situation. In that case, the growth rate would decrease significantly, alongside the exit multiple and margin expansion, becoming highly dependent on volume growth, which the company has no power to control.

The main question when discounting its future cash flows would be the timing of this happening, although I see a low chance of this happening (10% probability).

If a limit on price increases of 5% was imposed from 2027, assuming a lower growth volume, a 3% B2C revenue growth, and a 3% growth in software revenues, operating margins should also be adjusted.

In this scenario, I apply an 88% operating margin on the score segment and a 30% on the software segment, a tax rate between 22% and 24%, with a 3% growth in perpetuity and a 20x FCF exit multiple, obtaining an average fair price of $730 per share.

Risks

The two main risks I see for FICO's development are the regulation on its price increases, given that I believe the company has pushed prices up fast in a short period, and a change in regulation regarding the requirements for selling loans to the GSEs.

Price Increase Regulation

Even I see low chances of a short-term regulation regarding price hikes given it is a B2B business and the credit bureaus have been able to pass price increases due to the low costs compared to the overall mortgage origination costs, I believe the company has recently attracted some attention.

In October 2024, 34 Democratic senators and representatives submitted a letter urging the previous U.S. President to direct the Department of Justice to investigate FICO.

Given the change in presidency and the newly appointed FHFA president, I believe some of the fears might be dissipating given the deregulation approach of the new government.

GSE Mortgage Score Regulation

The second most relevant risk is the possibility of a change in regulation regarding the requirements to sell a mortgage to the GSEs.

While the FHFA has postponed indefinitely the transition to the bi-merger report and the use of Vantage Score for government-backed mortgages, over the long term there could be the possibility that consumers can choose between the use of one or the other score.

While I don't believe giving total power to the credit bureaus would benefit the industry, since having a third party as FICO providing the score has proven to be efficient, it is a risk worth keeping an eye on.

Conclusions

By assigning a 10% chance on the pessimistic scenario, a 20% on the most optimistic scenario, and a 70% chance to my base case scenario, the current fair price stands at $1,760.

While some risks that could significantly impact FICO's performance if they materialize, I don't assign them a high chance taking into account the mortgage originations market structure and the constant delays from the FHFA in regard to the bi-merge report implementation.

I acknowledge the current overvaluation of the company, but given the high quality of the business and the margin for further price increases over the next years, I will increase my position as the stock falls in the $1,500-$1,700, assigning it a buy rating.

data.title: Meta's Bullish Rally Is Far From Over
data.description:
Meta’s (NASDAQ:META) recent earnings have reinforced the narrative that the company is in a better position to monetize its investments in AI. The diluted EPS grew by a staggering 50% compared to the year-ago quarter. Meta is showing strong revenue growth as well as margin expansion which has helped boost the EPS growth momentum for the company. A key metric to look for in Meta is the YoY growth in price per ad. In the recent quarter, price per ad showed 14% YoY growth which shows better ad targeting by Meta. Good growth in this metric can increase the margin and overall EPS for the company in the next few quarters. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Meta could deliver a strong performance as new AI tools come online.

Meta is also losing close to $20 billion in Reality Labs on an annualized basis. This is a massive investment, and we are seeing new competition from Samsung in addition to Apple’s (AAPL) VR efforts. This segment showed revenue of $1.08 billion, which is a modest 1% YoY growth. Meta will need to increase the sales performance of this segment in 2025 to justify the huge losses and to maintain its market share as new devices are launched in this segment.

Despite the big bull run of the last two years, Meta stock is still reasonably valued when we compare it with other big tech peers like Apple. Meta’s forward PE multiple is 24.2 for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. On the other hand, Apple is trading at over 28 times the EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Sep 2026. Meta is also showing better revenue and EPS growth momentum than Apple, which should help the stock gain a better sentiment over the next few quarters.

Meta’s rapid monetization of capex

A few days before the earnings, Mark Zuckerberg announced a plan to ramp up capex to a range of $60 billion to $65 billion in 2025. The company is building massive data centers and has 1.3 million GPUs powering its AI tools. The top range of Nvidia’s (NVDA) GPU costs as much as $40,000 which shows the huge investment required to build this much computing power. This announcement came prior to the DeepSeek shock, but the management has reiterated its plans to maintain the current capex trajectory.

Figure: Meta's latest earnings beat. Source: Seeking Alpha

Wall Street has been more positive towards Meta’s capex plans compared to other big tech companies. We recently saw a big correction in Alphabet (GOOG) stock post-earnings, with the higher capex seen as a negative for the stock. Meta has received more positive reviews for its capex plans because of the quick monetization of the AI tools built by the company. These new AI tools are helping Meta deliver more targeted ads to customers which increases the return on investment for advertisers.

Figure: Key metrics for Meta in the recent earnings. Source: Meta filings

The overall revenue for Meta’s Family of Apps increased to $47.3 billion from $39 billion in the year-ago quarter. This is a 21% YoY growth in revenue. Meta reported good growth in all metrics. The daily active people or DAP grew 5% YoY, ad impressions increased by 6% YoY and the average price per ad increased by 14% YoY. Despite the higher investments, Meta managed to increase the margins, which has helped the EPS grow by 50% YoY.

AI impact on price per ad

A key metric to look out for is the price per ad of Meta. Over the last few quarters, Meta has been launching new AI tools for its advertisers. This allows Meta to do better targeting of ads and helps increase the ROI of advertisers. Better ROI for advertisers in turn helps Meta increase the price per ad.

Figure: YoY percentage change in Price per ad for Meta. Source: Meta filings

We can see from the above image that Meta has been able to steadily increase the price per ad percentage change over the last few quarters. In the recent quarter, the price per ad metric showed 14% YoY growth worldwide and 12% YoY growth in the important US & Canada region.

There is an upper limit to how many ad impressions Meta can deliver without making its platforms saturated by ads. However, Meta should be able to increase the price per ad for a long time. Better AI tools and better ROI for advertisers should allow Meta to increase the rates it charges advertisers.

Figure: Increase in capex by Meta. Source: Meta filings

Despite the massive capex announced by the management, Meta could still deliver a good monetization trajectory for these investments. Cheaper and open-source models like DeepSeek should also help improve the return on investment for Meta. The rapid growth of DeepSeek also proves that Meta’s open-source strategy can deliver better results over the long term.

Loss in Reality Labs and new competition

Meta reported another quarter of increase in loss for its Reality Labs segment. The loss for Reality Labs increased from $4.6 billion in the year-ago quarter to $4.96 billion in the recent quarter. Meta is now showing close to $20 billion in annualized losses in the Reality Labs segment. Back in 2014, Meta purchased Oculus for $2 billion, and since then, the company has invested heavily in this segment. On the other hand, the YoY revenue growth for Reality Labs is a mere 1%.

Figure: Revenue and losses in Reality Labs. Source: Meta filings

The losses in this segment could try the patience of most long-term investors. There is also higher competition as Samsung/Google announced the first Android XR headset. The Samsung device, called Project Moohan, is expected to cost under $1,500 making it much more affordable than Apple’s Vision Pro.

It could be a few more years before we could expect the Reality Labs segment to reach anywhere close to breakeven. This is a big risk for the Meta stock, and it is unlikely that the management is going to reduce the current investment scale in this segment. The positive impact of Reality Labs is that it provides Meta with complete control over the device and platform, which it can monetize to a maximum limit through its social media apps. The privacy policies of Apple and other OEMs limit Meta’s monetization ability. Reality Labs will provide devices and platforms that are completely controlled by Meta and this improves the growth runway for the company.

Reasonably priced stock

Over the last two years, Meta stock has increased from under $100 to over $700. This massive growth would seem like the stock is priced for perfection. However, the forward PE multiple of Meta is still quite reasonable when we compare it with other big tech peers. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2025, Meta’s forward PE is 27.9 and for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 Meta’s forward PE is 24.2.

Figure: Meta’s forward EPS estimate. Source: Seeking Alpha

This is significantly better than Apple and many other big tech peers, which are trading at higher multiples. Apple’s forward PE for fiscal year ending Sep 2026 is 28 which is 20% higher than Meta. We have also seen Meta deliver much stronger revenue growth in the last two years compared to stagnant revenue by Apple. The forward revenue and EPS growth estimates of Meta are much better than Apple, which makes it a more reasonably priced stock at the current price.

Figure: Comparison of Meta and Apple's price and forward EPS.

A lot of future stock price growth in Meta will depend on the ability of the company to effectively use the current investment in AI chips and build better tools for advertisers. If Meta continues to show double-digit price-per-ad growth for the next few quarters, we could see strong bullish momentum in the stock. This could possibly improve the forward EPS estimates, making the stock more attractive.

Investor Takeaway

Meta’s recent earnings report delivered another beat in revenue and EPS. The most important metric for the company is the strong price per ad growth which shows that it is able to do better targeted ads on its platform. We will likely see more AI tools by Meta to help advertisers reach their ideal audience. The capex announcement is quite high by Meta’s management, but Wall Street might accept this rate if Meta could continue to deliver strong revenue and margin growth.

Despite the bullish run of the last two years, Meta stock is reasonably priced when we compare the forward PE multiple of Meta and other big tech peers like Apple. Meta is trading at close to 24 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2026 which is 20% lower than Apple’s 28 times forward PE multiple.

data.title: US Foods Holding: Rating Downgrade On Cautious FY 2025 Outlook
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on US Foods Holding (NYSE:USFD) in Mar'24, for which I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that USFD is in a good position to continue growing EBITDA faster than revenue because of strong execution and positive underlying demand, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. For 4Q24, USFD delivered a strong quarter. However, I am downgrading to a hold rating because growth assumptions for 2025 feel a bit aggressive, particularly in independent restaurant case volumes. While I acknowledge the potential for further upside if demand recovers, I see the risk of USFD missing guidance, leading to multiple deratings.

Investment thesis

On 13th Feb 2025, USFD released its 4Q24 earnings, which saw revenue of $9.49 billion, up 6.2% y/y, primarily driven by total case volume growth of 3.5%. Within it, the independent restaurant segment saw organic case volume growth of 1.8%. Healthcare and hospitality grew cases by 4.7% and 2.4%, respectively. Adj gross profit grew 7.2% to $1.66 billion, with adj EBITDA up 13.7% y/y to $441 million (30bps expansion in EBITDA margin to 4.6%). Consequently, adj EPS came in at $0.84, up ~30% y/y.

One of the biggest positives for USFD is the visible EBITDA margin expansion runway ahead, and this was very apparent in USFD FY24 performance. Starting with the internal levers that management pulled: (1) Strategic vendor management initiatives drove $70 million in cost savings in 2024 (management's next target is $260 million in cost savings by 2027); (2) Digital investments like the MOXe e-commerce platform have enhanced customer engagement. Independent restaurant adoption of the platform has reached 77%, leading to larger basket sizes and improved retention, and (3) route optimization through Descartes' routing technology has driven >2% in distribution productivity improvements. Collectively, these initiatives have yielded tangible results: gross margin expanded 17 bps y/y, adj EBIT margin expanded 34 bps y/y, adj EBITDA margin expanded 30 bps y/y, and adj net margin expanded 30 bps y/y-enabling USFD to grow earnings at a faster rate than revenue.

Another area that I see as a catalyst for FY25 EBITDA margin expansion is USFD progress in private-label penetration, which reached 52% in 2024 (the goal is 54.5% by 2027, as per USFD's investor day). My view is that private-label expansion is a critical driver of margin improvement. Independent restaurants (~33% of revenue) are USFD's most profitable customers (per 10K filing), and management is targeting 4-7% independent case growth in FY25 with the expectation that the industry will see a recovery in restaurant foot traffic. If demand rebounds, this could serve as a meaningful tailwind to both revenue and EBITDA growth.

Our most profitable customers are independent restaurants. We tend to work closely with independent restaurant customers, providing them access to our customer value-added tools, and as a result are able to earn a higher operating margin on sales to them. These customers are also more likely to purchase our private label products, which are our most profitable products. Source: USFD 10K

The other positive aspect about USFD is that it has been disciplined in capital allocation. In 2024, USFD repurchased $958 million in shares (16.4 million shares, or ~6.6% of FY23 shares), and I think USFD can continue to support this level of buybacks while investing in growth. For note, on top of the buybacks, USFD 2024 CAPEX totaled $341 million (used for fleet expansion, warehouse automation, and technology investments). As of FY24, USFD has a net debt to EBITDA level of 2.8x, which is within management's target range, and consensus is expecting USFD to generate ~$860 million FCF in FY25. There is definitely flexibility for USFD to conduct a similar level of buybacks and CAPEX if they want to in FY25 (remember EBITDA grew as well, so that pushes the leverage ratio down in FY25).

What makes me turn less bullish is that while USFD performed well in 4Q24, I cannot shrug off the idea that underlying demand for the independent restaurant category is weakening-case growth only saw 1.8% in 4Q24. As I noted above, management is guiding for 4-7% independent case growth in 2025, assuming a rebound in foot traffic. This is a steep acceleration that I do not have confidence about. The fact is that restaurant traffic (on an industry-wide basis) remains poor. According to Blackbox Intelligence, restaurant traffic has remained poor, and my thought is that consumer discretionary spending will get worse due to the uncertain macro environment (lingering inflation and potentially higher rates). Hence, while management expects improvement, to me, this is a "show-me" story that may not turn out as expected.

Valuation

The main reason for my emphasis on growth expectation is because USFD now trades at 11x forward EBITDA, 1x above its historical average (and above where I wrote previously). While this 1x may seem little, let me point out that USFD has historically traded in a very tight range of ~9x to 11x, and the current multiple is at the high end of its historical trading range. There have only been a few instances that USFD traded above this level: late 2019, the Covid period, and early 2022. Putting aside the covid period, every time it barely touches above 11x, multiple derates very quickly back to the historical average. With the risk that USFD may miss its FY25 revenue growth guidance and consensus EBITDA estimates (10% EBITDA growth estimate), I see a real risk of multiple deratings. For me to gain confidence in the USFD equity story again, I need to see solid improvement in independent restaurant case growth in 1Q25 (it has to be up at least mid-single-digits for management guidance to be credible).

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for USFD is a hold rating. While I see the upside case for the business, I downgraded my rating because of the potential of USFD missing its FY25 guidance and consensus estimates, which may drive multiples downwards. USFD needs to show that independent restaurant case growth can accelerate to mid-single-digit percentages in 1Q25; if not, it will be hard to convince me that the full-year expected growth can be achieved.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Thesis Busted?
data.description:
Introduction

If you are a The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) shareholder, as I am, I hope you could sleep well Wednesday night, or you didn't choke on your Thursday morning coffee when you found out the price action after the company announced its Q4 2024 earnings results.

The stock dropped by 34.4% since the earnings. Ouch.

This was the very first time in 33 quarters, since the company went public, that The Trade Desk had missed its own guidance.

On top of that, guidance for Q1 2025 was disappointing.

If you combine these elements, the question becomes if this is a pivotal moment and The Trade Desk has lost its magic. Or is it just a temporary bump in the road?

I think it's pretty safe to say that I know The Trade Desk better than 99.9% of the investors. I've followed it intensely since I picked it as a Potential Multibagger in May 2019 at (a split-adjusted) $19.5.

Since then, I've written more than 150 times about The Trade Desk for the subscribers of Potential Multibaggers, from short news to deep analysis and everything in between.

I think I can say I know a thing or two about this company, so I want to critically analyze whether this is a turning point for The Trade Desk or just a blip on the screen.

The numbers

In Q4, The Trade Desk's revenue grew 22.3% year-over-year to $741 million, but it's a miss of $18.55 million versus the consensus. More importantly, The Trade Desk also missed its own guidance of 'at least $756 million.'

That information is crucial if you want to understand the big stock price drop. For the first time since its IPO 33 quarters ago, The Trade Desk missed its own guidance, by about 2%. Normally, the company beats the consensus by 2% on average. This time, it missed it by almost 3%.

The non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.59, beating the estimates by $0.02. This shows that The Trade Desk has leveraging power. Even with a revenue miss, the company can squeeze out more earnings. On a GAAP basis, EPS came in at $0.36, up 81.5% year over year.

The big difference between GAAP and non-GAAP is still caused by the stock grant plan for Jeff Green, but that's stopping in 2025, so GAAP and non-GAAP should be much closer to each other. The $90 and $115 levels triggered his sizeable stock-based compensation. The next price trigger is $145.

The company also issued another $1 billion for the stock repurchase plan.

With the company's extremely strong financial position, this is no problem at all. It has $1.9 billion in cash and short-term investments and with the free cash flow, we end up at $2.1 billion in the most recent quarter. The Trade Desk has no debt.

For the eleventh consecutive year, The Trade Desk saw more than 95% customer retention.

Guidance for Q1 FY25 is “at least” $575M or 17% growth year over year, lower than the 18% growth consensus. The guidance missed the consensus by 0.9%.

The Call: The Jeff Green Monologue

The Trade Desk's founder and CEO Jeff Green is someone who's clearly passionate about his industry. If you want a masterclass in ad tech, just listen to all of Green's earnings calls. He always takes the time to share new insights, trends, and fundamentals of the industry. I wish all CEOs would share so much wisdom and insight.

And he's not a man who sugarcoats things.

First, he stressed that the full year 2024 was another strong year for The Trade Desk, but then he immediately said this.

I want to acknowledge upfront that for the first time in 33 quarters as a public company we fell short of our own expectations.

During COVID we revised our expectations once along with the rest of markets, but for the first time in 8 years we missed the expectations we set, and it was our fault.

(All the quotes are from the Q4 2024 earnings call)

The Trade Desk has always exceeded expectations, and Jeff Green nailed so many predictions that I found risky to share. When Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) came out saying it would stop third-party cookies, Jeff Green said they would never be able to do that within the time frame they wanted. I found that a very bold and risky prediction. I mean, this is Google, right?

But Green was right. Not only was the date postponed multiple times, in the end, third-party cookies were not stopped.

And, by the way, Green made another Google prediction this quarter. Here it is:

We are preparing for a world where Google exits the open internet. I’m confident that one way or another Google will exit the open internet. I think they should. Most of their anti-trust and regulatory problems come from the draconian ways they have engaged with the open internet in the past. (...)

If and when Google exits the Open Internet, they will leave a big hole and a big opportunity for the rest of us.

Let's see if this is another correct prediction.

I could give many other examples from the past where Green made these bold predictions, and each time, he was right. But that's beyond the scope of this article. I just wanted to show that because I have followed Jeff Green so closely for so many years, and he has proven time and time again to be trustworthy.

And that's why I believe him when he says:

Starting off, let me explain it as I see it, what falling short of our own expectations does NOT represent. This didn’t happen because the opportunity isn’t as big as we thought. In this case, it isn’t because of competition either.

It's essential that Green emphasizes that this is not about a competitive threat. Because that's also something I saw in several comments. "Amazon (AMZN) is eating their lunch!" or "AppLovin is stealing market share." Jeff Green denies this, and I believe him. Not just because I'm naively following everything he says, but because it makes sense.

Why would Amazon suddenly become a stronger competitor, while it has been into this space for years already? Green on Amazon:

More and more the only competitors we encounter today have the worst objectivity problems. Amazon is asking advertisers big and small for their advertising budgets. Meanwhile, Amazon competes with most of the Fortune 500 companies in some way, whether we are talking about Microsoft in cloud, or P&G in CPG products, or UPS, or Nike, or all the rest.

Jeff Green has been saying this for years. Google and Meta (META) 'get to grade their own homework' (in other words, there's no certainty that the numbers they give you as an advertiser are 100% correct), and indeed, Amazon competes with many brands. The Trade Desk is an independent player and was focused on being one from the start.

AppLovin (APP) isn't a significant threat to The Trade Desk right now because their focus is different. AppLovin dominates in mobile gaming and in-app ads and there is definitely some competition for mobile ads, but The Trade Desk is more in brand advertising. Or, to put it differently, The Trade Desk serves the big brands and ad agencies, AppLovin the smaller ones. That doesn't mean it can't become a strong competitor, of course. After all, AppLovin has seen impressive growth.

But they also have a partnership with The Trade Desk, use UID2, the standard developed by The Trade Desk, and are fully active on The Trade Desk's platform.

So, what is the real reason for the disappointing results, then? According to Jeff Green:

For Q4, the reality is that we stumbled due to a series of small execution missteps while simultaneously preparing for the future.

For Q4, the reality is that we stumbled due to a series of small execution missteps while simultaneously preparing for the future. (...)

Simply put, as you have seen before, as companies grow and become increasingly complex, they need recalibration to unlock new opportunities. We are recalibrating our larger company for an even stronger future.

Green didn't want to go into specifics, as it would expose people who made honest mistakes, and moved on:

And when we talk about the missteps specifically, many of them involve people, mistakes that aren't appropriate to discuss publicly, especially when people are already learning from these mistakes.

He also mentioned that there were a few instances where the company could have chosen for short-term performance but chose for long-term opportunities. Implicitly, there may have been the implication that The Trade Desk could have met its guidance if it wanted, but it didn't do this because they didn't want to sacrifice long-term goals for short-term gains.

And management already changed a few things to right the ship. Four main points were mentioned.

1. Reorganizing

In December, The Trade Desk did the largest reorganization in its history so far. Green:

While we often make structural changes at the end of the year to improve our business, this was bigger than usual. For most people in the company, we provided a much clearer view of their roles and responsibilities, and for most that also meant a change in reporting structure. (...)

2. Internal Effectiveness

The Trade Desk always saw such a big market that it totally focused on that. Now, it has become of such a size, it also needs to focus internally. Green:

Over the past two months, leadership has spent more time discussing operational improvements than at any other point in our history. While we’ve historically been focused on external opportunities, we understand that this moment requires us to scale our internal operations and continue hiring senior talent to support long-term growth.

3. More Focus On Brands

Our commitment to agencies remains strong, but we are also expanding brand-direct relationships, particularly through Joint Business Plans, which grow 50% faster than the rest of our business.

That last part is essential. The Trade Desk started working with brands like Walmart, Home Depot, Amazon, and many other giants. Ad agencies are intermediaries and with technological revolutions, like with AI now, you often see that the middlemen are cut out. While they are still a very powerful partner of The Trade Desk, the company's also prepared for a future in which they play a less important role.

4. Revamping The Product Development Process

Green mentioned that the product teams had become too big. The Trade Desk reorganized its software teams into smaller scrum teams again instead of "waterfall methods," which are inherently slower. Green:

Our engineering team is now divided into nearly 100 scrum teams with a system to more easily ship and collaborate with the business team on what has shipped and what will ship and when

Green also talked CTV (Connected TV) being "the kingpin of the Open Internet." The reason is that nearly 100% of viewers are logged in. He even claims:

CTV should be the first place all brand advertisers spend, not walled gardens.

That's a bold statement, but it makes sense. If you advertise on Google or Meta's apps, you can only see the numbers they give you. When people are all logged in to CTV, the measurability of ads is so much better and more accurate.

And what's often used for this measurability on CTV? UID2, the standard developed by The Trade Desk, which is now run by an independent organization.

Green emphasized that CTV is the fastest-growing channel, even if it is already the biggest one. He says that neither The Trade Desk nor content owners think the saturation point is anywhere close.

Smaller still, but a big opportunity, according to Green, is audio. He called it 'still the most on-sale corner of the Open Internet.'

I think that's true, and it's probably true it's still a big opportunity.

Of course, just like on every call from any company nowadays, there was attention for AI as well:

We started our ML and AI efforts in 2017 with the launch of Koa, but today the opportunities are much bigger. We’re asking every scrum inside of our company to look for opportunities to inject AI into our platform. Hundreds of the enhancements recently shipped and coming in 2025 would not be possible without AI.

The difference is that The Trade Desk has indeed been an early adopter of AI and ML (machine learning).

Green emphasized another feature, one that the competition, be it Amazon (outside its own ecosystem) or AppLovin in the future, doesn't have:

In Kokai, we have the industry’s richest retail data environment, including data from many of the world’s leading retailers to help advertisers understand the connection between campaign spend and consumer action.

This has always been the holy grail of advertising: being able to measure in the stores how your advertising campaign worked. With The Trade Desk, that's possible. And I don't think any other retailer would be willing to give these data to Amazon.

Green also said that The Trade Desk would simplify the platform:

We will simplify our platform. As platforms mature, they add features but that can make it more complex. We will continue to add features and powerful controls for the most sophisticated buyers in the world. However, we are finding ways to improve the experience and make decisions easier and more intuitive for our users.

That's great. Most great leaders do this simplification exercise regularly.

All in all, Green outlined 15 initiatives that The Trade Desk will focus on. That also means the company will invest in VP-level and above executives and infrastructure. Green named the function of COO, for example.

Capex will remain low, though, at just 5% of revenue. But there will be impact on the EBITDA margin.

The Call: Q&A

As Green talks for so long and already explains so much, the Q&A is often less interesting for The Trade Desk.

Of course, the first question was about the miss. Green basically answers the same thing, with restructuring and new initiatives. But there was also this very candid admission:

So first, let me own that we missed and that we missed our own expectations, as you point out, which is in my mind, very different from missing Wall Street's expectations.

So when we set our guide and set our expectations, I view that as a commitment.

It's understandable in a moment like this for those outside the company, especially shareholders to be wondering what does this mean? Is the opportunity not as big as The Trade Desk claims? Or is it different than what they thought? Is the company not executing? Is there something wrong, if so, is it big or is it small?

I just want to be super clear, we missed because we had a series of small execution missteps.

Green also gave an example of what went wrong and why it was a decision that gave up short-term gains for long-term opportunities:

One of those -- you're right, that Kokai rolled out slower than we anticipated. But much of that was for good reason. We've seen moments and places to inject AI like improving the foundation of our forecasting and performance models. That is a short-term negative for sure, but it is a long-term negative. We are working -- I'm sorry, it's a long-term positive.

Green also talked about how the political campaigns had an effect not observed before to that extent: companies holding their budgets because they know there will be a flood of political campaigns they could drown in.

Political put some advertisers on the sidelines, that's absolutely true. But it also brings out budgets, especially, of course, the political budgets. And on the net, was it a positive or a negative? To me, it's too close to call.

In the past, political spending was always a tailwind for The Trade Desk. But now, other brands paused their spending. And that effect was big enough to offset the political spending. I think many overestimated political spending. It accounted for about 5% at the peak in Q4.

Green also went deeper into his comments about Google and Amazon. If you are interested, you can read the transcript here.

My Take

In honor of Jeff Green, I won't sugarcoat this either. This was a disappointing quarter, and it's understandable that The Trade Desk's stock was taken to the woodshed.

For months already, I shared with the subscribers of Potential Multibaggers that I found The Trade Desk's stock too expensive. With a valuation score of 4/10, I think that's pretty clear.

I'll review the quality and valuation scores next week, as I think this is a crucial moment. But overall, I feel pretty confident for the long term.

The Trade Desk's valuation will be much more attractive after this, especially because Jeff Green mentioned re-acceleration multiple times in the call. I see no reason not to believe him.

You see a lot of dramatic statements about The Trade Desk now, but the fact stands that this has consistently been one of the best companies out there.

Every great company goes through crises. It's how they are handled that matters.

Think about Netflix (NFLX) and the Qwikster debacle, Amazon's stock crash of 95% in the dotcom crash, Meta and the Cambridge Analytica scandal, NVIDIA (NVDA) missing its own guidance in Q3 2018, etc.

Great leaders use crises to improve their companies. Jeff Green is a great leader, one of the best I know. So why would I doubt him now?

Of course, the pessimism may even become stronger over the next few weeks and months and the problems may not be solved in one quarter. It could take two or, who knows, three. But never underestimate a determined visionary leader. Troubles make them even more determined to show the world what they are capable of.

The Trade Desk didn't wait and started handling the problems right away.

Also, don't forget that, despite the weaker fourth quarter, revenue was up 26% for The Trade Desk in2024. Programmatic advertising will only become bigger and The Trade Desk is the leader of programmatic advertising for the Open Internet. It has no conflicts of interest, and it innovates continuously.

This is not the first time The Trade Desk's stock price has dropped so much. It dropped by 28% just 5fivequarters ago. Did you still know?

What I mean is that if this quarter is just a one-off slip, it will not matter in the long term.

Disappointing quarters are inevitable, even for the very best companies. The Trade Desk's high valuation makes the drop more spectacular.

I don't believe the fundamentals are in danger at this moment, though. Yes, there's some disappointment now, but that's mainly because the valuation was so high and there's little room for weak quarters like this one. Well, weak, many companies would still give an arm and a leg for these results, but in the context of The Trade Desk, it's weak. That alone already says something about this company and the standards its held to.

I hope the stock goes down even further. The $1 billion earmarked for buybacks will do its work, and I can load up more shares.

Don't forget that for a multibagger, how long the company grows is much more crucial than how fast it grows at any given moment. Or put differently: an earnings miss won't matter that much. It will be in the fundamentals over the long term.

So, I'll end with one last quote from Jeff Green. It seems appropriate to let him have the final say, also because it summarizes my own thoughts pretty well.

I am not happy with our results in the fourth quarter but there is so much opportunity in 2025 and the years ahead to help our clients take full advantage of data-driven advertising on the premium internet, to drive growth and brand loyalty for their businesses.

And that’s why I’m confident, The Trade Desk will eventually resume acceleration and continue the path we’ve been on for over 33 quarters as a publicly traded company.

You can expect the quality score and valuation score updates next week.

In the meantime, keep growing!

data.title: Astria Therapeutics: A High-Conviction Bet On HAE
data.description:
Small-cap biotech shares can be volatile, but Astria Therapeutics (NASDAQ:ATXS) has a particularly attractive risk-reward profile that the market seems to be underestimating. Astria has entered Phase 3 trials for its hereditary angioedema lead candidate Navenibart (STAR-0215) and has designed a second program, STAR-0310 for atopic dermatitis. The company is poised for an important inflection point. Yet what sets it apart in today's market is its healthy cash position relative to its burn rate, a factor that could give it an extended runway when many small biotechs are struggling to raise money without dilutions.

Financials

Astria’s financial state is a hidden strength in a biotech landscape where liquidity concerns are a major headwind. It had $344.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments as of September 30, 2024, up from $246.5 million as of 2023. Those cash reserves give Astria something many clinical-stage biotech companies lack: flexibility. While capital markets have tightened and secondary offerings smother share prices, Astria is positioning itself to fund operations through mid-2027 without needing to raise additional cash immediately.

Net operating cash burn of $28 million was reported in Q3 2024, with an annualized burn rate of $112 million. Simple math says Astria has about three years of runway at its current burn rate, assuming no material change in expenses. This is well above the expected Phase 3 readout for Navenibart in early 2027, a key inflection point for the company. Expenses may rise as trials progress, but the existing cash balance buffers dilution risk for investors - something that's not so for many of its small-cap biotech peers competing for funding. The last few months have, for the most part, been consistent in developments with the past. Astria continues R&D, and there have been no new surprises in terms of unforeseen expenses. In about three weeks, the company will release the official number, but I imagine their burn rate should be relatively consistent.

The company is losing money despite that cushion. For Q3 2024 Astria posted a $24.5 million net loss, up from $17.7 million in Q3 2023. This increase in cost is mostly attributed to the Phase 3 preparations for Navenibart and the ongoing STAR-0310 program, including manufacturing and trial initiation. It is necessary, but it means the company growth thesis only remains valid if it can complete its pivotal trials successfully. The upside here is that management has targeted spending efficiently - the vast majority going to R&D rather than administrative bloat. Unlike some biotech firms that see disproportionate general and administrative costs as they scale, Astria's G&A expenses grew only from $6.9 million to $8.5 million year-over-year while remaining focused on clinical execution.

Drug Pipeline

Astria's pipeline centers on two monoclonal antibody programs, Navenibart for hereditary angioedema and STAR-0310 for atopic dermatitis. Both have market potential, but Navenibart is the clear near-term value driver. In the Phase 3 ALPHA-ORBIT trial, which begins in Q1 2025, dosing arms are designed to be every three months and every six months, a patient-centric strategy that may represent the most convenient approach to prophylactic HAE treatment on the market. Early results from the Phase 1b/2 ALPHA-STAR trial demonstrated a 90-95% reduction in mean monthly attack rates and a 67% attack-free rate at six months, suggesting best-in-class potential. These results along with positive pharmacokinetic and safety data make this pivotal study likely to succeed. Although there are competitors in the HAE space, current treatments require more frequent dosing or lack long-term data matching Navenibart's profile, so differentiation may be possible if Phase 3 confirms prior results.

A Phase 1a trial of STAR-0310 began in January 2025 to evaluate safety and pharmacokinetics in healthy adults in early-stage clinical development. This asset targets OX40, which is a key pathway in atopic dermatitis and other inflammatory diseases, and is targeted as a potential best-in-class therapy with YTE half-life extension technology. With lower antibody-dependent cell toxicity than a competitor in Phase 3, rocartinlimab, the company believes it will lead to a better safety profile and therapeutic window. It is too early to make Star-0310 a real value proposition, but initial proof-of-concept data expected in Q3 2025 may act as an upside catalyst if early differentiation becomes clear.

Scientific Background and Approach

Excessive bradykinin production causes hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by severe, unpredictable swelling attacks. Attacks on the airway may be life-threatening and require prophylactic treatment. Currently, available therapies either replace the C1-inhibitor, which regulates kallikrein activity, or directly inhibit kallikrein to prevent bradykinin overproduction.

The monoclonal antibody against plasma kallikrein, Navenibart, is a long-lasting inhibitor that requires minimal dosing. Unlike other inhibitors that require biweekly or monthly administration, Navenibart's extended half-life permits dosing every three or six months, reducing the treatment burden. Drug durability has improved with advances in antibody engineering without compromising efficacy - an important issue in patient adherence.

Atopic dermatitis is characterized by itching, redness and persistent lesions caused by T-cell dysfunction and barrier disruption. OX40 signaling is a key pathway, which enhances T-cell activation and survival, in memory T-cells of chronic inflammation.

Astria's second asset, STAR-0310, is an OX40 antagonist monoclonal antibody that blocks excessive T-cell activation while maintaining normal immune function. With YTE (Xtend) half-life extension technology, it requires less frequent dosing than most other OX40 inhibitors. Its reduced antibody-dependent cell cytotoxicity suggests a safer profile with lower off-target toxicity - an issue that has challenged some immune-modulating therapies in dermatology.

Phase 3 Strategy Signals Confidence in Navenibart

The ALPHA-ORBIT Phase 3 trial design represents a step toward regulatory approval and market positioning for Astria. Three-month and six-month dosing regimens will be tested in the study, which supports the company's contention that dosing flexibility could provide a competitive advantage over more frequently administered treatments.

This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial will enroll 145 patients with Type 1 or Type 2 HAE across global sites. Time-normalized monthly attack rates at six months are the primary endpoint, with attack-free rates as a secondary measure. These metrics mirror real-world treatment goals and increase the likelihood of regulatory approval if results are similar to those from prior trials. Open-label extension will allow patients to continue treatment, provide long-term safety and efficacy data, and ensure trial retention.

The trial will start in Q1 2025 with top-line results by early 2027. Although the timeline is long, two dosing regimens and a robust statistical analysis plan suggest a focus on regulatory strength over speed to market. This study was developed with input from the FDA and EMA indicating its global approval potential.

If navenibart can maintain a 90-95% reduction in attack rates with twice-yearly dosing, it could dramatically change treatment preferences. For patients and physicians, a lower treatment burden often determines market leadership - and Astria is betting on that trend.

STAR-0310 Enters Trials With an Eye on Differentiation

The launch of STAR-0310 by Astria is its first major step into the competitive field of atopic dermatitis therapies in which biologics are increasingly redefining treatment paradigms. STAR-0310 competes with established players such as Sanofi's Dupixent (SNY) and a wave of new immunomodulators, unlike Navenibart, which operates in a relatively defined niche market. A big differentiator is in its mechanism of action and extended half-life, two features it says could provide both efficacy and convenience advantages over current options.

The OX 40 pathway, which STAR-0310 targets, is critical for T-cell survival and chronic inflammation, and thus a natural target for AD driven by dysregulated immune responses. Its unique YTE half-life extension technology makes STAR-0310 a long-term active agent in the body, which may result in less frequent dosing than competitors. Such a difference may be meaningful as patients and providers increasingly seek out therapies that reduce treatment burden. Preclinical data also indicates lower antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity of STAR-0310 than rocatinlimab, another OX40 antagonist in Phase 3. This discrepancy could lead to a better safety profile and a larger therapeutic window, two factors that have often determined the commercial viability or failure of immunotherapies in dermatology.

Safety, pharmacokinetics, and immunogenicity in 40 healthy adult participants are being evaluated in the Phase 1a trial, which began in January 2025. This early-stage study is not designed to demonstrate efficacy but will provide important information about how the drug behaves in the human body that will help inform future trials in AD patients. With the guidance of early proof-of-concept data in Q3 2025, Astria has outlined a gradual but deliberate development.

Balancing Innovation With Execution Risks

The success of Navenibart is dependent on maintaining near-complete kallikrein inhibition with minimal dosing, but the translation of early-stage data into clinical benefit and regulatory approval remains unclear. While Phase 1b/2 data suggested a 90-95% reduction in attack rate, larger trials typically exhibit variability in response, unexpected safety issues, or regulatory hurdles. The placebo control of the ALPHA-ORBIT trial should clarify if these attack-free rates are valid, but HAE is a highly individual disease. Any deviation from prior efficacy benchmarks would erode its positioning. Antibody-based therapies also involve immunogenicity risk, and although Navenibart showed a good profile, long-term tolerability remains a question.

The challenge for STAR-0310 lies in differentiation in a competitive atopic dermatitis market. AD is regulated by multiple inflammatory pathways, so blocking OX40 alone may not provide broad disease control. Directly targeting key cytokines such as IL-4/IL-13 inhibitors like Dupixent already dominate the space. Other investigators argue that OX40 plays a more restricted role in AD and that STAR-0310 may benefit only a subset of patients rather than being a universal therapy. The YTE half-life extension may allow less frequent dosing but prolonged drug exposure may limit flexibility in case of safety issues.

Of course, both programs also face commercial viability risks. The HAE is a defined rare disease market whose size limits potential sales, and insurers will dictate the uptake of Navenibart. If those who pay see only minor advantages over existing treatments, they may impose access restrictions. In AD, STAR-0310 must outperform existing biologics and also prove its place in an evolving market. Skepticism about the role of OX40 in AD indicates that STAR-0310 may be effective, but will not replace current standard treatments unless safety and dosing benefits are compelling.

Valuation and Market Share

In terms of raw numbers and metrics, the company has a price-to-book ratio of 1.62 compared to the sector median of 2.23, which is a good sign in terms of the company’s financial management despite being early stage. Other than this, however, Astria has very little to show in the way of meaningful financial ratios. This is because the company has no products that have reached market yet, so there is no actual revenue from its products yet. For biotech companies at this stage, their potential value is largely determined by two factors: the science behind the drugs, and the total addressable market if successful. While I have extensively discussed the former, I will estimate the latter below.

About 1 in 50,000 people have HAE. For the US, which has about 330 million people, that amounts to roughly 6,600 people with HAE. HAE is also very economically burdensome. A prior study analyzing data before 2008 reported an average annual cost per patient of $44,597, including direct medical costs and indirect costs like lost productivity. This works out at about $65,000 annually per patient adjusted for inflation through 2025. Newer analyses show that modern HAE therapies can be quite expensive. Hence, one study reported annual medication costs of $363,795 per patient, excluding additional indirect costs of $52,576.

From these figures, the total addressable market for HAE treatments in the U.S. can be estimated. From the estimated 6,600 patients with a $363,795 average annual treatment cost, we arrive at a TAM of approximately $2.4 billion annually, although Astria themselves estimate it higher at about $2.8 billion. This estimation is for the US market only; With regard to the global prevalence of HAE, the worldwide market potential is much greater.

Astria Therapeutic's lead candidate, Navenibart, aims to compete in this market by providing a potentially best-in-class prophylactic treatment for HAE. The suggested dosing regimen of once every three to six months could be advantageous compared to other therapies whose dosage is more frequent. If Navenibart shows superior efficacy and safety in its Phase 3 trials, it may become a preferred treatment option and potentially capture a large share of the HAE therapeutic market.

Takeaways and Outlook

Astria Therapeutics combines clinical ambition with financial discipline in a biotech sector where many companies struggle to achieve both. Its lead candidate, Navenibart, has a clear regulatory path, a defined market, and a dosing advantage that could reshape the treatment of hereditary angioedema if Phase 3 results confirm early data. The three-month and six-month dosing regimens represent a patient-centric approach that if successful may increase market penetration and payer acceptance. In the meantime, STAR-0310's entry into the atopic dermatitis market is a riskier but potentially valuable secondary asset that will need to differentiate itself to establish itself in a competitive space.

Astria is unique among clinical-stage biotechs in having a healthy cash position versus its burn rate that permits execution on both programs with no immediate dilution risk. That means this financial runway is available through mid-2027 - a luxury few small-cap biotechs can afford in order to fund pivotal trials. But the company still values itself on the basis of clinical execution - and investors will want to see whether the Phase 3 trial maintains efficacy and attack-free rates from earlier studies. STAR-0310 is still far from being validated, and the OX40 inhibition approach will need to establish a distinct advantage over existing therapies to gain traction.
